facts_disclosed: Noma stage cancelled — they closed, and she can't afford the trip anyway

turns.content: it's done. finished. they closed the restaurant. Redzepi announced it months ago and I kept telling myself maybe the stage program would continue in some form, maybe they'd do pop-ups, maybe something. but no. it's over. the whole thing is shuttered

I remember the closure announcement hit the whole food world hard. I'm sorry — if you'd been holding onto hope that the stage program might survive in some form, having that door close completely must be crushing.

and you know what, even if by some miracle they reopened tomorrow and said "Nadia, come do your stage" — I looked at what the trip would actually cost. flights to Copenhagen, hotels for two weeks, I wouldn't be earning anything while I'm there. blyat. I can't afford it. I was delusional to think I could

That's two blows at once — the opportunity itself disappearing, and then realizing the finances wouldn't have worked anyway. That second part can almost feel worse, because it means even the fantasy version wasn't realistic.

exactly. the dream is dead. both ways. the restaurant doesn't exist and even when it did exist I couldn't have made it work. I spent months imagining myself in that kitchen, learning their fermentation techniques, working with those Nordic ingredients. I used to fall asleep thinking about it. and now it's just... nothing
Grieving a dream you've been carrying for that long is real grief — it doesn't matter that it was a plan and not a memory. You'd built a whole future around it in your head. That's not nothing.

I keep thinking I should feel more philosophical about it. like "oh, one door closes another opens" or whatever. but I don't feel that. I feel bitter. I feel like the universe dangled something perfect in front of me and then yanked it away. putain. I'm angry

You don't have to be philosophical about it right now. Bitter and angry are honest responses. You applied, you committed emotionally, you planned — and forces completely outside your control took it away. That's infuriating. You're allowed to sit in that for a while.

my coworker asked me today if I'd heard back about the stage and I just said "it's not happening, don't bring it up." probably too harsh but I can't talk about it right now without wanting to throw something. the whole Noma chapter is closed. I need to move on

That's not too harsh — it's a boundary. You told them clearly where you stand and that's fine. Moving on doesn't mean you have to be okay with it yet. It just means you stop planning around something that isn't there anymore.

yeah. yeah exactly. stop planning around it. I need to focus on what's actually in front of me. the kitchen I'm in, the menu we're building. not some fantasy in Denmark. merde. I really wanted it though

Of course you did. It was a legitimate dream and you had every reason to want it. The fact that you're redirecting your energy toward your actual kitchen doesn't erase that it mattered. Let yourself be angry tonight. Tomorrow you cook.

tomorrow I cook. that's the only thing that's ever been consistently true in my life. okay. I'm going to open a bottle of wine and be sad for exactly one more hour and then I'm done with this. Noma is dead. long live Noma. moving on
One hour, one bottle, and then forward. That sounds like the right ritual for this. Goodnight, Nadia.
